metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add AC/test coverage for verify_authorship parse/shape failure paths: malformed DSSE payload (non-JSON body), Statement missing required fields (empty subject, absent digest key), and key-lookup failure — each must yield a non-verified result and must not raise, matching the plan's explicit promise.
findings.finding: The plan's What section explicitly promises 'Any parse/shape failure or mismatch yields a non-verified result; verify NEVER raises on lookup error,' but no Acceptance Criterion tests this behavior. A malformed DSSE payload (non-JSON, truncated), a Statement missing required fields (_type, subject array empty, digest key absent), or a key-lookup failure are all new user-facing failure modes introduced by this story that remain untested.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (AC3) / What section: verify_authorship behavioral promises
findings.suggested_fix: Add a new AC bullet: 'verify_authorship returns a non-verified result (never raises) when given: (a) a DSSE payload that is not valid JSON, (b) a Statement with an empty or malformed subject array, (c) a Statement missing _type or predicateType, (d) a key-lookup error for the signing identity. A parameterized test covers all four cases.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_commands/identity.py` to `file_impact` (or confirm `verify_authorship` keeps its current call-compatible `(envelope, identity_id)` interface and document how `_verify_keyop_signature`'s existing call stays valid under the new design).
findings.finding: `verify_authorship`'s proposed new signature `(event, envelope, trust_root)` is a breaking interface change that will silently break the existing caller in `identity.py:411` — which calls `authorship.verify_authorship(signature, identity_id, repo_root=repo_root)` — but `identity.py` is absent from `file_impact`.
findings.location: ## What / `verify_authorship(event, envelope, trust_root)` description; file_impact section; `src/rebar/_commands/identity.py:411`
findings.suggested_fix: Either: (a) keep `verify_authorship`'s public interface as `(envelope, identity_id, *, repo_root=None)` and move content-binding logic into the caller (`verify_authorship.py`) only, OR (b) add `identity.py` to `file_impact` and update `_verify_keyop_signature` to pass `(event={}, envelope=signature, trust_root=resolved_trust_root)` — but option (a) is safer since the key-op path has its own independent content-binding check (`signature.payload != expected`).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `identity.py` to `file_impact` (or explicitly document in the plan that key-op envelopes use a different code path that is unaffected), and clarify whether `authorship.verify_authorship` itself validates in-toto Statement structure or whether that check is only in `verify_authorship.py`'s `_verify_signed`.
findings.finding: `identity.py` is an unenumerated consumer of `authorship.verify_authorship` that the plan's changes will affect. `_verify_keyop_signature` (identity.py:411) calls `authorship.verify_authorship(signature, identity_id, ...)` for KEY-ADD/KEY-REVOKE authorization, where `signature.payload` is raw `keyop_payload(...)` bytes — not an in-toto Statement. If the reworked `verify_authorship` (or its internals) validates that the DSSE payload is a valid in-toto Statement, key-op verification will fail silently or raise. `identity.py` is absent from `file_impact`.
findings.location: file_impact section; src/rebar/_commands/identity.py:389–416
findings.suggested_fix: Explicitly state in the plan that `verify_authorship` (in authorship.py) does NOT parse the Statement payload — it only verifies the DSSE signature. The in-toto Statement parse + subject binding check is the responsibility of `verify_authorship.py`'s `_verify_signed`. This keeps `verify_authorship` safe for key-op callers in `identity.py`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state whether key-op signing (KEY_ADD/KEY_REVOKE via `keyop_payload`/`_verify_keyop_signature`) is in-scope for the Statement transition or whether it retains a raw-bytes payload — and if retained, confirm `sign_authorship`'s new signature matches `_verify_keyop_signature`'s expectation or that a separate signing entry point is used.
findings.finding: The plan reworks sign_authorship to always wrap an in-toto Statement payload, but identity.py's _verify_keyop_signature (outside the stated file_impact list) compares signature.payload directly to raw keyop_payload bytes — this path will silently break after the transition unless the plan clarifies whether key-op signing bypasses the new Statement wrapping or _verify_keyop_signature is also updated.
findings.location: What section ('sign_authorship produces … Statement'); file_impact (identity.py listed only for create_placeholder); src/rebar/_commands/identity.py lines 389–413 (_verify_keyop_signature)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the Scope or What section: 'key-op signing (KEY_ADD/KEY_REVOKE) uses a separate entry point (`keyop_payload` + raw-bytes DSSE) and is NOT transitioned to the Statement format; sign_authorship is for event-authorship only.' Then confirm _verify_keyop_signature is not broken by listing it explicitly as out-of-scope with a rationale.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State in AC4 (or a named test-fixture note) the exact configuration the end-to-end test must set to activate signing through `_seam.py` (e.g., `identity.require_authenticated = True` + a valid `key_path`) so the roundtrip genuinely exercises the new Statement payload path rather than the Tier-0 unsigned fallback.
findings.finding: AC4's end-to-end test description does not specify how the test enables the opt-in signing path through the write seam, even though the plan states signing is default-off (`identity.require_authenticated` default off). A test that does not explicitly configure the signing path could pass without ever invoking `sign_authorship` through `_seam.py`, making the 'proving the raw-bytes compare path is gone' claim unverifiable.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4 / 'Consumer that MUST change' section
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC4: 'The test fixture configures `identity.require_authenticated = True` and supplies a test signing key via `key_path`; the write-seam call must return a signed event (not Tier-0), confirmed by asserting `event["author_sig"]` is present before passing the event to `verify-authorship`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify (and fix) which layer owns Statement content-binding: either move the `subject[0].digest.sha256` ↔ recomputed-hash check INTO `verify_authorship` / `verify_authorship_at_epoch` (making it a library guarantee), OR explicitly document that content-binding is the caller's responsibility and update the plan's 'What' description to remove the implication that the library function does it.
findings.finding: The plan's 'What' section states verify_authorship '(2) checks subject[0].name == event_uuid AND subject[0].digest.sha256 == recomputed content_hash', implying the library function enforces Statement content-binding. But the actual merge-gate calls verify_authorship_at_epoch, which delegates only to registry.verify (DSSE crypto — no Statement parse). Statement content-binding is instead placed in verify_authorship.py._verify_signed. verify_authorship_at_epoch is NOT listed for changes in file_impact. This creates a gap: any caller that invokes verify_authorship_at_epoch directly bypasses content-binding, and the plan is internally inconsistent about which layer owns it.
findings.location: What section — verify_authorship description; src/rebar/attest/authorship.py verify_authorship_at_epoch (line 130); src/rebar/_commands/verify_authorship.py _verify_signed (line 114)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `_check_statement_binding(envelope, event)` helper in authorship.py that parses the Statement payload and asserts `subject[0].name == event['uuid']` and `subject[0].digest.sha256 == content_hash(event minus author_sig)`. Call it from both `verify_authorship` and `verify_authorship_at_epoch` before delegating to `registry.verify`, so the content-binding guarantee is at the library layer regardless of caller.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a post-rollback schema-state assertion: document what state persisted `author_sig` blobs and SNAPSHOT `authorship_ledger` entries are left in if the landed stack is reverted, and either (a) describe a compensating-forward procedure to re-verify/reclassify them, or (b) confirm that the revert path also includes a script/step to purge or rewrite the new-format blobs before the old verify code goes live.
findings.finding: The plan's no-migration argument ('the stack is unlanded, so NO persisted event carries an old-format author_sig') is a point-in-time assertion at first landing but the plan never asserts the schema STATE AFTER ROLLBACK. Once the stack lands and events begin accumulating new-format in-toto Statement author_sig blobs (and those blobs are folded into SNAPSHOT compiled_state.authorship_ledger by compact.py), a revert of the landed stack leaves new-format Statement envelopes permanently baked into committed JSON files. The old verify logic (raw-bytes compare and ledger re-verify path) cannot parse these blobs, and the plan provides no back-out path for that state. This is the exact scenario the T11(e) 'POST-ROLLBACK SCHEMA STATE' clause requires the plan to address.
findings.location: Landing / sequencing section; Scope section ('no migration/backfill is required'); also src/rebar/_commands/compact.py _build_authorship_ledger and src/rebar/_commands/verify_authorship.py _ledger_events

findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Co-locate the full 3-step content-binding protocol (parse Statement from envelope.payload → sha256(canonical-event-minus-author_sig) → compare to subject[0].digest.sha256) in one place, and explicitly state whether `_ScopedEvent.payload` retains its current `bytes` semantics or changes.
findings.finding: The processing protocol for the `verify_authorship.py` content-binding update is split across two sections without specifying what `_ScopedEvent.payload` becomes under the new scheme. The Consumer section says the check 'reads subject[0].digest.sha256', but the plan never explicitly states whether `ev.payload` remains canonical event bytes (with sha256 applied inline in `_verify_signed`) or is repurposed. An implementer must infer a 3-step protocol (parse Statement from envelope.payload → extract sha256 → compare to sha256(ev.payload)) across two sections, with no single co-located description of all three steps.
findings.location: Consumer that MUST change / verify_authorship.py content-binding check; _verify_signed in verify_authorship.py line 127
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'Consumer that MUST change' section, add: 'ev.payload retains its current semantics (canonical event bytes, author_sig excluded). _verify_signed extracts subject[0].digest.sha256 from the parsed Statement and compares it to hashlib.sha256(ev.payload).hexdigest().'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the return type of `create_placeholder` to AC6 (e.g. 'returns the identity id str, consistent with ensure_identity_for').
findings.finding: `create_placeholder`'s return type is unspecified. The plan says it is a 'thin idempotent alias over ensure_identity_for' — which returns `str` (the identity id). The AC says 'mints a keyless placeholder identity, idempotent on repeat (test)' but doesn't state what the function returns, which is needed for any caller that uses the return value.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC6; create_placeholder rationale section
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC6: 'returns the identity id (str); idempotency is asserted by calling twice with the same args and checking the returned ids are equal.'

findings.finding: The plan changes `author_sig` from raw canonical bytes (existing bde5 behavior) to a DSSE-encoded in-toto Statement, which is a breaking format change for any consumer that currently reads or verifies `author_sig`. The Scope section names era model (e165) and ledger/verdict (3183) as out-of-scope follow-on stories, but the plan provides no expand-contract sequencing, feature flag, or rollback/back-out path to keep the system functional between this story landing and those stories landing.
findings.location: Scope section; Why section; file_impact _seam.py

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Rewrite AC1's test-assertion clause to explicitly name BOTH the DSSE `payloadType` field AND the Statement `predicateType` field so a test author asserts each independently (e.g., 'a test asserts the envelope `payloadType`, the Statement `_type`, and the Statement `predicateType` values').
findings.finding: AC1 ends with 'a test asserts the structure + payloadType' but the parenthetical in the same bullet lists `predicateType` (a Statement-level field) as the field under test. 'payloadType' is the DSSE envelope field; 'predicateType' is the in-toto Statement field. A test author following the AC's closing clause could check only the DSSE payloadType and miss asserting the Statement's predicateType (or vice versa).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC1
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the closing clause of AC1 with: 'a test asserts (a) the DSSE envelope's `payloadType == "application/vnd.rebar.authorship.v1+json"`, (b) the Statement `_type == "https://in-toto.io/Statement/v1"`, and (c) the Statement `predicateType == "application/vnd.rebar.authorship.v1+json"`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the verdict label (e.g. BAD_SIGNATURE) emitted for each Statement parse/shape failure mode (JSON parse error, _type mismatch, subject shape invalid, name mismatch, sha256 mismatch) so the verify path's non-verified returns are unambiguous.
findings.finding: The verdict label emitted for each Statement parse/shape failure in the `verify_authorship` (and `verify_authorship_at_epoch`) path is undefined. The plan says 'any parse/shape failure or mismatch yields a non-verified result' but the existing code uses a specific string vocabulary (BAD_SIGNATURE, UNKNOWN_AUTHOR, etc.) and the AC tests only assert 'FAILS on a tampered event' without naming which label is expected.
findings.location: What section — verify_authorship bullet; AC line 3 ('FAILS on a tampered event (payload/content_hash mismatch)')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the verify_authorship bullet: 'All Statement parse/shape/mismatch failures emit BAD_SIGNATURE (consistent with the existing tampered-event label); no new verdict labels are introduced.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly note in the _seam.py file_impact (or the sign_authorship bullet) that the pre-computation of `payload` bytes is removed and the call site is updated to pass the event dict.
findings.finding: The new `sign_authorship(event, key_path, identity_id)` signature (event dict replaces payload bytes) implies the call site in `_seam.py` must change from pre-computing `payload` bytes to passing the event dict, but `file_impact` lists `_seam.py` only as 'stamp author_sig = DSSE Statement envelope' — it does not name the parameter change at the call site. The existing call `authorship.sign_authorship(payload, signing_key, principal=str(author_id))` (line 302, _seam.py) would break silently if the new function still accepts bytes (wrong input) or loudly on a type error.
findings.location: file_impact — src/rebar/_commands/_seam.py; What section sign_authorship bullet

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan whether the reworked `sign_authorship` takes an `event: dict` (and handles author_sig exclusion + content_hash internally) or still takes `payload: bytes` (and wraps it in a Statement), and update the `file_impact` / seam description accordingly.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `sign_authorship` will have a new signature taking an `event` dict (`sign_authorship(event, key_path, identity_id)`), but the current seam call-site `_seam.py:302` passes pre-computed `payload: bytes` — the seam update is in `file_impact`, but the plan never explicitly reconciles whether the new `sign_authorship` accepts a raw event dict OR still accepts bytes (with the Statement wrapping done inside). This ambiguity leaves implementors to guess the boundary and risks the seam and authorship module diverging.
findings.location: ## What / `sign_authorship(event, key_path, identity_id)` description; `src/rebar/_commands/_seam.py:299-302`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan whether `verify_authorship_at_epoch` is also updated (it is the merge-gate's actual verifier), or document explicitly that Statement-parse + content-binding stays in `verify_authorship.py`'s `_verify_signed` while both `verify_authorship` and `verify_authorship_at_epoch` remain pure signature-verification functions.
findings.finding: `verify_authorship_at_epoch` in `authorship.py` (line 130) is called from `verify_authorship.py:135` and is the actual signature-verification entry point for the merge-gate, but the plan's `What` section describes changes to `verify_authorship(event, envelope, trust_root)` with a different signature and new Statement-parsing responsibility — neither `verify_authorship_at_epoch` nor its relationship to the new Statement-aware contract is mentioned. If Statement-awareness is pushed into `authorship.verify_authorship` but not `verify_authorship_at_epoch`, the merge-gate's epoch-scoped check lacks the content-binding.
findings.location: What section — `verify_authorship` description; src/rebar/attest/authorship.py:130–156; src/rebar/_commands/verify_authorship.py:135

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the 'What' section (write seam description) an explicit specification of the key-unavailability fallback: how `_seam.py` detects that a signing key is absent/unavailable and what it writes in that case (e.g., omit `author_sig`, write Tier-0 unsigned event), so AC5 has an implementation description to map to.
findings.finding: AC5 ('An unavailable signing key yields an unsigned (Tier-0) write, never a blocked write') covers a fallback behavior that is never described in the 'What' section's specification of `sign_authorship` or the write seam (`_seam.py`). There is no described code path for how the write seam detects key unavailability and downgrades to an unsigned event, leaving AC5 as an orphaned criterion with no implementation target.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC5; What section (sign_authorship, write seam)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback statement to the 'Landing / sequencing' section — e.g., 'Back-out: if submission fails mid-stack, revert all submitted changes in reverse order; since no data was written in the new format (opt-in, no production traffic), revert is safe and complete' — so T4's reversibility requirement is met.
findings.finding: The plan changes the `author_sig` wire format (a breaking change) and justifies skipping a rollback plan by stating the stack is unlanded and submission is atomic, but never explicitly names the back-out procedure. T4 requires an explicit rollback/back-out plan or expand-contract sequencing, not merely an acknowledgement that breakage is contained.
findings.location: Landing / sequencing section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expand AC5 and the `_seam.py` spec to state that ANY exception from `sign_authorship` (not just key-unavailable) causes a Tier-0 unsigned fallback, and add a test covering at least one non-key-unavailable failure mode (e.g., malformed key file).
findings.finding: The write seam's graceful-degradation scope is underspecified: only 'unavailable signing key' is named as a fallback trigger, but other signing failures (malformed key file, filesystem I/O error, JSON encoding exception, SSHSIG library error) are not explicitly handled. These could propagate as unhandled exceptions instead of degrading to an unsigned (Tier-0) write.
findings.location: AC5 / `src/rebar/_commands/_seam.py` / 'An unavailable signing key yields an unsigned (Tier-0) write, never a blocked write'
findings.suggested_fix: Update the plan to read: 'Any failure in `sign_authorship` (key unavailable, key malformed, I/O error, codec error) yields an unsigned (Tier-0) write, never a blocked write. The seam wraps `sign_authorship` in a broad try/except and logs the failure reason before falling back.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add observability requirements to the plan: specify that signing failures/fallbacks are logged at WARN with reason, Statement parse failures are logged at DEBUG with the offending payload shape, and (optionally) a counter metric incremented on each fallback/rejection.
findings.finding: No observability instrumentation is specified for the new failure points introduced by this story: signing failures in the write seam, Statement parse/shape failures in `verify_authorship`, and content_hash mismatches. Operators have no visibility into how often signing degrades or why verification rejects events.
findings.location: ## What / `verify_authorship` spec / `_seam.py` spec / AC5

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the idempotency behavior of the `_seam.py` stamp: either (a) if `author_sig` is already present, skip re-signing, or (b) always overwrite (and document why that is safe), and confirm whether the write is atomic with the event record.
findings.finding: The idempotency of the `_seam.py` stamp operation is not specified: if `sign_authorship` is called twice on the same event (e.g., due to a retry), the plan does not define whether the second call re-uses the existing `author_sig`, overwrites it, or produces a different signature (SSHSIG signatures may be non-deterministic).
findings.location: ## What / `_seam.py` spec — 'stamps `author_sig` = the DSSE-encoded Statement envelope'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether the epic AC literally requires the symbol name `create_placeholder` to exist in the public API, or whether satisfying the AC through the already-public `rebar.ensure_identity_for` (which has identical semantics and 4+ existing call-sites) is sufficient — if the latter, drop `create_placeholder` to avoid Rule-of-Three surface proliferation.
findings.finding: `create_placeholder(provider, external_id, display_name)` is proposed as a new public entry-point with zero existing call-sites, as a pure alias over `ensure_identity_for` which already has 4+ public call-sites (`rebar.__init__`, `rebar._lib_writes`, `rebar._engine/rebar_reconciler/apply_inbound_records.py`, and tests). This is a premature abstraction (Rule-of-Three: < 3 new callers) and a config/surface proliferation of an already-public symbol.
findings.location: Plan section 'create_placeholder rationale' and AC line 6: `create_placeholder(provider, external_id, display_name)`; file_impact: `src/rebar/_commands/identity.py`
findings.suggested_fix: If the AC requires the name for external compatibility, add it as a module-level alias in `_lib_writes.py` with a one-line docstring pointing to `ensure_identity_for` and no separate implementation, and document it as a compatibility shim rather than a distinct function. If the AC does not mandate the exact name, remove it entirely and cite `rebar.ensure_identity_for` in the AC test.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive rationale sentence to the 'What' section explaining why `event_uuid` belongs in `subject[0].name` (e.g. 'in-toto subject is the attested artifact; the event IS the artifact being attested, so its UUID is the subject name and its SHA-256 content_hash is the subject digest — matching in-toto's intended semantics').
findings.finding: The plan assigns event_uuid to `subject[0].name` — an in-toto v1 field designed for resource URIs/artifact paths (e.g. OCI image refs, file paths) — without stating why this field is the right semantic home for a rebar event UUID vs. placing event identity in the `predicate` object (which is explicitly for custom claims). The plan gives no positive rationale for this structural mapping choice.
findings.location: What section — Statement schema (exact); subject field description

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the secret-lifecycle posture for `identity.signing_key` on the CI path: specify that in CI it MUST be injected via a secrets manager / CI secret (not a committed config value), and audit the `exc_info=True` signing-failure log in `_apply_authorship` to ensure the key path cannot appear in CI log output (e.g. redact the path from the logged exception or catch path-related OSError before the generic handler).
findings.finding: The plan introduces `identity.signing_key` (a path to a local SSH private key) as a config field that must be populated in CI for the merge-gate to sign events, but it does not declare the secret-lifecycle posture for this value in the CI deployment context — whether it should be injected as a CI secret/env var, mounted as a file, or stored in a secrets manager. An undeclared secret on the boundary-crossing (CI) path is itself the gap. Additionally, the existing `_apply_authorship` signing-failure handler logs `exc_info=True` (seam.py:309), which in a signing-failure scenario could surface the key path or key file contents in CI log output.
findings.location: What section / `_seam.py` `_apply_authorship` / `_config_schema.py` `identity.signing_key` / AC: unavailable signing key yields unsigned write
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note to the plan's 'Landing / sequencing' or 'Scope' section: '`identity.signing_key` in CI MUST be supplied via a CI secret (env override or mounted secret file), never as a committed config value.' In `_apply_authorship`, replace the bare `except Exception: logger.warning(..., exc_info=True)` with a targeted catch that logs a fixed message without the exception chain when the failure is an I/O or key-unavailability error, to prevent key-path leakage in CI logs.

findings.finding: The scope-exclusion claim 'OUT: ledger/verdict (3183 transition), which CONSUMES this story's content_hash + verify' is a false exclusion: verify_authorship.py already contains a hardcoded content-binding check (line 127: `envelope.payload != ev.payload`, where ev.payload is raw canonical bytes recomputed at line 203-205) that will immediately return BAD_SIGNATURE for every event signed under the new Statement payload format this story introduces. The file is not in file_impact and is explicitly excluded, yet the code change is load-bearing for it.
findings.location: Scope section: 'Out: era model (e165 transition) and ledger/verdict (3183 transition)'; verify_authorship.py:114–138 (especially lines 127, 203–205)
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) add verify_authorship.py to file_impact and update _verify_signed to extract content_hash from the Statement and re-derive the expected canonical bytes for comparison, or (b) explicitly gate this story on the 3183 update landing first (reverse the sequencing), since the two changes are mutually load-bearing.

findings.finding: `src/rebar/_commands/verify_authorship.py` is an unenumerated consumer that directly implements the OLD raw-bytes payload binding and will break when `sign_authorship` switches to an in-toto Statement payload.
findings.location: Scope / file_impact (absent entry); `src/rebar/_commands/verify_authorship.py` lines 200–205, 127
findings.suggested_fix: In `verify_authorship.py`, replace the raw-bytes `payload` reconstruction with a `content_hash` computation matching what `sign_authorship` embeds in the Statement subject, then check that the decoded Statement's subject `content_hash` equals that computed value instead of comparing `envelope.payload` directly to raw bytes.

findings.finding: verify_authorship.py's _verify_signed re-derives ev.payload as raw canonical event bytes and guards on envelope.payload == ev.payload (line 127); after the transition the envelope's payload is an in-toto Statement, so this guard will always fail for every live signed event, classifying them all as BAD_SIGNATURE. The file is absent from file_impact.
findings.location: file_impact section (absence of verify_authorship.py); cross-ref src/rebar/_commands/verify_authorship.py:127,200-206
findings.suggested_fix: In _verify_signed, after decoding the envelope, parse the Statement payload (JSON), extract subject[0].digest (or the content_hash field), recompute the expected content_hash from the event's canonical bytes minus author_sig, and compare those — instead of the current envelope.payload == ev.payload byte comparison.

findings.finding: `verify_authorship.py` is absent from `file_impact` but MUST change: it currently recomputes `payload` as raw canonical bytes (line 203) and compares `envelope.payload != ev.payload` (line 127). When the envelope payload becomes an in-toto Statement, the Statement JSON bytes ≠ raw canonical event bytes, so this content-binding check will always return BAD_SIGNATURE for every newly-signed event.
findings.location: ## file_impact (lists authorship.py, _seam.py, identity.py — omits verify_authorship.py); verify_authorship.py lines 127, 201–205

findings.finding: The in-toto Statement JSON schema is referenced throughout the plan ('Statement', 'subject', 'subject binds {event_uuid, content_hash}') but its field vocabulary is never defined — the _type/_statement_type URI, the subject array element structure ({name, digest: {algName: value}}), whether a predicateType/predicate is required, and which field carries content_hash vs. event_uuid are all unspecified.
findings.location: ## What — 'sign_authorship builds the Statement + content_hash'; AC line 1 'payload is an in-toto Statement … with a subject binding {event_uuid, content_hash}'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Statement shape' subsection to ## What, e.g.: `{"_type": "https://in-toto.io/Statement/v0.1", "subject": [{"name": "<event_uuid>", "digest": {"sha256": "<content_hash_hex>"}}], "predicateType": "application/vnd.rebar.authorship.v1+json", "predicate": {}}`; then reference this schema from both AC lines 1 and 2.

findings.finding: The plan changes `author_sig` from a raw-bytes signature to a DSSE-encoded in-toto Statement envelope (a persisted format change on event rows), but provides no migration, backfill, or backward-compatibility strategy for existing rows written with the old format. `verify_authorship` is specified to 'parse the Statement', implying it will reject all pre-existing author_sig values, with no rollback path and no statement of the data state after a partial deployment.
findings.location: ## What — `sign_authorship`/`verify_authorship` description; `author_sig` stamp in `_seam.py`; Scope section

findings.finding: The `_seam.py` cutover — stamping `author_sig` with the new DSSE envelope — is a live code-path change, but no AC exercises a full write-then-read round-trip through the seam to confirm the new author_sig is both written correctly and verifiable end-to-end. All four ACs test `sign_authorship` and `verify_authorship` in unit isolation; none triggers `_seam.py` to write an event and then verifies the resulting author_sig via `verify_authorship`.
findings.location: What section: 'The write seam (_seam.py) stamps author_sig = DSSE-encoded Statement envelope'; Scope: 'In: … author_sig on the write seam'
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: '[ ] Writing an event via the seam produces an `author_sig` that decodes as a valid DSSE envelope and passes `verify_authorship` against the signing identity (integration test covering `_seam.py`).`

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
